source_metadata.title_raw: JoAnn Yeo - A-82-003 - January 21, 1982 - Seal Beach

content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Administration 
322-5660 
Mrs. JoAnn Yeo, City Clerk 
City of Seal Beach 
211 8th Street 
Seal Beach, CA 
90740 
Dear Mrs. Yeo: 
Executive/Legal 
322-5901 
Enrorcement 
322-6441 
January 21, 1982 
Statements of Economic I nteres! 
322-6444 
This letter will conIlrm my advice to you during our telephone 
conversation of January 6, 1982. 
Under the Political Reform Act, a candidate is required to file 
campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Section 84200. 
If a candidate involved in an April 13, 1982 election receives 
and/or spends less than $500 for his cfu~paign, the candidate 
is required to file only the Officeholders and Candidates 
Campaign Statement, Short Form 470. 
However, if $500 or more 
is raised or spent on behalf of his candidacy, the candidate 
is then required to file the Officeholders and Candidates 
Campaign Statement, Long Form 430 listing all contributions 
received and expenditures made. 
I also advised you that an 
officeholder or candidate is not required to file a Statement 
of Organization Form 410 if he or she receives or spends more 
than $500 as long as all contributions received and all expend-
itures made are reported on the candidate's campaign statement 
Form 430. 
Finally, the term "contribution" as defined in 
Government Code Section 82015 does not include "a payment made 
by an occupant of a home or office for costs related to any 
meeting or fundraising event held in the occupant's home or 
office if the cost for the meeting or fundraising event is $500 
or less. II 
The term "contribution" also does not include 
"volunteer personal services or payments made by any individual ..• 
if such payments are made voluntarily without any understanding 
or agreement that they shall be directly or indirectly repaid." 
Therefore, if the candidate asks any person or group of persons 
to put window or 
yard signs up for his candidacy at their own 
personal expense, these signs are considered to be a "volunteer 
personal service" and are not reportable by the candidate as 
inkind or non-monetary contributions. 


Mrs. JoA..'1n Yeo 
Page 2 
However, if an individual were to construct several signs and 
solicit others to display the signs and the cost related to the 
construction of the signs was $500 or more, the individual would 
then qualify as an "expenditure committee" (Governoent Code Section 
82013(b)) and would then be required to file a Form 461 as an 
expenditure co~~ittee. 
I hope this information has been helpful to you. 
If I can be of any 
further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me at 
213-620-5196. 
Sincerely, 
--
-; 
Helen P. Arriola 
Political Reform Consultant 


December 30, 1981 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. o. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
Attention: 
Ms. JeanetteTurvill 
Dear Ms. Turvill, 
I am requesting your opinion regarding a detenmnation for 
reporting gifts from another agency. 
I am specifically 
referring to the League of California Cities, Orange COl.mty 
Division meetings whereby once a year city officials are 
provided with oorrplirnentary tickets to (1) the Laguna Beach 
Festival of Arts; and (2) an Angels baseball game. 
In rrost 
instances the tickets are provided for the city official and 
their spouse. 
The regular weekday price for tickets to the 
Arts Festival range from $10 to $20 per seat and I ass1..lITe the 
baseball tickets would be $10 or less each, havever the 
accumulati ve arrount for these events if the city official 
attended both with their spouse would exceed the $25 "gift" 
limi t , and asslID'ling that such activities oonducted as a 
league function are oonsidered reportable. 
Your opinion will be appreciated and if you have any questions, 
please do not hesitate ot call.

sections.question: I am requesting your opinion regarding a determination for reporting gifts from another agency. I am specifically referring to the League of California Cities, Orange County Division meetings whereby once a year city officials are provided with complimentary tickets to (1) the Laguna Beach Festival of Arts; and (2) an Angels baseball game. In most instances the tickets are provided for the city official and their spouse. The regular weekday price for tickets to the Arts Festival range from $10 to $20 per seat and I assume the baseball tickets would be $10 or less each, however the accumulative amount for these events if the city official attended both with their spouse would exceed the $25 "gift" limit, and assuming that such activities conducted as a league function are considered reportable.
sections.conclusion: Under the Political Reform Act, a candidate is required to file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Section 84200. If a candidate involved in an April 13, 1982 election receives and/or spends less than $500 for his campaign, the candidate is required to file only the Officeholders and Candidates Campaign Statement, Short Form 470. However, if $500 or more is raised or spent on behalf of his candidacy, the candidate is then required to file the Officeholders and Candidates Campaign Statement, Long Form 430 listing all contributions received and expenditures made. An officeholder or candidate is not required to file a Statement of Organization Form 410 if he or she receives or spends more than $500 as long as all contributions received and all expenditures made are reported on the candidate's campaign statement Form 430. The term "contribution" as defined in Government Code Section 82015 does not include "a payment made by an occupant of a home or office for costs related to any meeting or fundraising event held in the occupant's home or office if the cost for the meeting or fundraising event is $500 or less." The term "contribution" also does not include "volunteer personal services or payments made by any individual if such payments are made voluntarily without any understanding or agreement that they shall be directly or indirectly repaid." Therefore, if the candidate asks any person or group of persons to put window or yard signs up for his candidacy at their own personal expense, these signs are considered to be a "volunteer personal service" and are not reportable by the candidate as in-kind or non-monetary contributions.
sections.question_synthetic: Must city officials report complimentary tickets to arts and baseball events provided by the League of California Cities if the aggregate value exceeds the $25 gift reporting limit?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advises that campaign filing requirements depend on the amount raised or spent, with candidates raising/spending less than $500 filing Form 470 and those exceeding $500 filing Form 430. Volunteer personal services and certain event-related costs are not reportable as contributions under specific conditions outlined in Government Code Section 82015.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two separate communications: incoming request from December 30, 1981 regarding gift reporting from League of California Cities, and FPPC response letter dated January 21, 1982 addressing campaign statement filing requirements. The response does not directly address the gift reporting question from the incoming request; instead it covers campaign filing rules. This appears to be a compilation of related but distinct communications. Confidence reduced due to mismatch between incoming question topic and FPPC response topic.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I am requesting your opinion regarding a determination for reporting gifts from another agency. I am specifically referring to the League of California Cities, Orange County Division meetings whereby once a year city officials are provided with complimentary tickets to (1) the Laguna Beach Festival of Arts; and (2) an Angels baseball game. In most instances the tickets are provided for the city official and their spouse. The regular weekday price for tickets to the Arts Festival range from $10 to $20 per seat and I assume the baseball tickets would be $10 or less each, however the accumulative amount for these events if the city official attended both with their spouse would exceed the $25 "gift" limit, and assuming that such activities conducted as a league function are considered reportable.

CONCLUSION: Under the Political Reform Act, a candidate is required to file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Section 84200. If a candidate involved in an April 13, 1982 election receives and/or spends less than $500 for his campaign, the candidate is required to file only the Officeholders and Candidates Campaign Statement, Short Form 470. However, if $500 or more is raised or spent on behalf of his candidacy, the candidate is then required to file the Officeholders and Candidates Campaign Statement, Long Form 430 listing all contributions received and expenditures made. An officeholder or candidate is not required to file a Statement of Organization Form 410 if he or she receives or spends more than $500 as long as all contributions received and all expenditures made are reported on the candidate's campaign statement Form 430. The term "contribution" as defined in Government Code Section 82015 does not include "a payment made by an occupant of a home or office for costs related to any meeting or fundraising event held in the occupant's home or office if the cost for the meeting or fundraising event is $500 or less." The term "contribution" also does not include "volunteer personal services or payments made by any individual if such payments are made voluntarily without any understanding or agreement that they shall be directly or indirectly repaid." Therefore, if the candidate asks any person or group of persons to put window or yard signs up for his candidacy at their own personal expense, these signs are considered to be a "volunteer personal service" and are not reportable by the candidate as in-kind or non-monetary contributions.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter A-82-003 from January 21, 1982, addresses campaign statement filing requirements for candidates in the April 13, 1982 election and clarifies what constitutes reportable contributions under the Political Reform Act. The letter explains thresholds for different forms, volunteer service exclusions, and when expenditure committees must be formed.
